title: Are Choice Experiments Incentive Compatible?
text:
Preface
This site reproduces, in R, the main empirical results of
The original analysis was conducted in LIMDEP. This is a replication project for AGEC 5045 — Applied Discrete Choice Analysis (NTU, 114-2), written as a Quarto book so that code, results, and commentary sit side by side and the whole analysis can be reproduced end to end.

title: Are Choice Experiments Incentive Compatible?
text:

title: Are Choice Experiments Incentive Compatible?
text:
How to read it

Data preparation runs live in the book — the Data chapter reads the raw spreadsheet and builds the estimation dataset in front of you.
Model estimation code is shown but loaded, not re-run. The heavy models (custom-likelihood MNL scale test, HEV quadrature, simulated-ML RPL, the Poe bootstrap) are estimated by the numbered scripts in R/, saved to output/*.rds, and loaded here; results are then recomputed for display. This keeps the book fast and deterministic while still showing every line of code.
Commentary is set off in coloured callout boxes so my own notes are never confused with the paper’s claims or the raw output.







Reproducibility



All code and data are in the GitHub repository. To rebuild from scratch, run the scripts in R/ in order (see Reproducibility) and re-render the book.


The headline result, stated once up front: hypothetical choices overstate real willingness to pay. Every qualitative finding of the paper replicates; the quantitative gaps that remain are documented and interpreted in the final chapter.

title: 1  The original study
text:
1.1 Research question
Choice experiments (CEs) ask respondents to pick repeatedly among multi-attribute products at varying prices, and have become a workhorse for valuing food quality, environmental goods, and new products. They are widely believed to be more incentive compatible than open-ended contingent valuation because they mimic real shopping. Lusk & Schroeder (2004) test that belief directly: do the choices people make — and the willingness-to-pay (WTP) values recovered from them — differ when the task is hypothetical versus when real money changes hands?
This is the same “hypothetical bias” question long studied in the contingent valuation literature, but moved inside the choice-experiment framework. The paper’s distinctive contribution is to pin down which WTP measures are biased: marginal WTP for a quality change, or total WTP relative to a no-buy option.

title: 1  The original study
text:
1.2 Empirical design

Good. 12-oz ribeye steaks differentiated by a quality/assurance label: Generic, Guaranteed Tender (GT), Natural, USDA Choice, and Certified Angus Beef (CAB), plus a “None” (no-purchase) option — six alternatives.
Choice task. Each subject faced 17 choice scenarios; in each, the five steaks were offered at experimentally varied prices ($3.38–$9.00) and the subject chose one steak or “None”. The full price design is in the paper’s appendix and is reproduced in the Data chapter.
Treatments (the key manipulation).

Nonhypothetical (real): subjects knew that one scenario would be drawn at random and binding — they would actually buy the steak they chose at the posted price. Scenario 17 (all five steaks at the same $5.63) served as the binding scenario.
Hypothetical: identical task, but no transaction ever occurred.

Estimation (LIMDEP in the original). A sequence of discrete-choice models on the panel of choices: multinomial logit (MNL); a test of IIA via the universal (“mother”) logit; and three less-restrictive models — heteroscedastic extreme value (HEV), multinomial probit (MNP), and random parameters logit (RPL). Treatments are compared with the Swait–Louviere scale-and-preference test, and WTP differences are tested with the Poe combinatorial test.

title: 1  The original study
text:
1.3 Key findings of the paper

Hypothetical bias is present. Hypothetical respondents choose the no-purchase option less often and exhibit higher WTP than real-payment respondents.
The error scale differs across treatments, and a joint model rejects equality of preferences across treatments.
IIA is violated for the pooled and hypothetical data (universal-logit LR test), motivating the HEV / MNP / RPL models.
Bias is concentrated in total WTP (value of a steak vs. buying nothing) rather than in marginal quality premiums — an important, nuanced result for practitioners.







My framing



The paper is essentially a careful measurement-validity exercise for stated preference methods. For replication this is attractive because every headline number is a likelihood, a test statistic, or a WTP — all things we can recompute from the raw choices. The challenge is less “what to compute” than “reproducing 2004 LIMDEP defaults in modern R,” which is where most of my discrepancies arise (see Discrepancies).

title: 2  Data structure and preparation
text:
2.1 The raw file
The authors provided harrison.xls. The relevant sheet, trt 10&11, is a wide spreadsheet — one row per respondent — with a two-row header, label rows separating the two treatments, and summary rows at the bottom. Treatment 10 is the nonhypothetical group; Treatment 11 is the hypothetical group.
raw_dims <- dim(readxl::read_excel("harrison.xls", sheet = "trt 10&11",
                                   skip = 3, col_types = "text"))
cat("Raw sheet (after skipping 3 header rows):",
    raw_dims[1], "rows ×", raw_dims[2], "columns\n")
#> Raw sheet (after skipping 3 header rows): 129 rows × 57 columns
Key variables.
Choice codes: 1 = Generic (GEN), 2 = Guaranteed Tender (GT), 3 = Natural (NAT), 4 = USDA Choice (CHO), 5 = CAB, 6 = None (NONE).

title: 2  Data structure and preparation
text:
Block
Columns
Contents




Identifiers
subject, session
Subject ID (1001–1099 = nonhyp; 1100–1199 = hyp)


Demographics
gender, age, education, student, income, …
Used for Table 1


Attitudes
color, tenderness, flavor, …
Survey Likert items (not used in estimation)


Choices
sc1–sc17
Chosen alternative per scenario, coded 1–6

title: 2  Data structure and preparation
text:
2.3 Cleaning: from spreadsheet to respondents
I keep only valid subject-ID rows (1001–1199), which automatically drops the treatment-label rows and the trailing summary rows. One subject (ID 1060) has all 17 scenarios missing and is excluded — this reconciles the 68 rows in the file with the 67 nonhypothetical subjects the paper reports.

demo_names <- c(
  "subject","gender","age","household","child","education","income","student",
  "occupation","race","gbeef","steak_buy","other_beef","gbeef_freq","steak_freq",
  "color","brand","quality_gd","ex_fat","in_fat","price_imp","safety","juiciness",
  "flavor","tenderness","consistency","doneness","production","quality_gd2",
  "slaughter","food_safe","quality_pref","cooked","chance1","chance2","chance3",
  "chance4","chance5")
sc_names <- paste0("sc", 1:17)

raw <- read_excel("harrison.xls", sheet = "trt 10&11",
                  col_names = c(demo_names, sc_names, "blank", "session"),
                  skip = 3, col_types = "text")

df_wide <- raw |>
  mutate(subject = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(subject))) |>
  filter(!is.na(subject), subject >= 1001, subject <= 1199) |>
  filter(subject != 1060) |>                              # all scenarios missing
  mutate(treatment = factor(if_else(subject < 1100, "nonhyp", "hyp"),
                            levels = c("nonhyp", "hyp"))) |>
  mutate(across(all_of(c(setdiff(demo_names, "subject"), sc_names, "session")),
                as.numeric))

cat("Nonhypothetical subjects:", sum(df_wide$treatment == "nonhyp"), "(paper: 67)\n")
#> Nonhypothetical subjects: 67 (paper: 67)
cat("Hypothetical  subjects:",  sum(df_wide$treatment == "hyp"),    "(paper: 37)\n")
#> Hypothetical  subjects: 37 (paper: 37)

title: 2  Data structure and preparation
text:
2.4 Reshape: wide → long → choice format
Two reshapes turn the respondent-level table into the estimation data:

Long — one row per respondent × scenario; merge in the five prices.
Choice (mlogit) format — one row per respondent × scenario × alternative, with a logical chosen flag and the alternative’s own price. Scenario 17 is excluded from estimation (it is the all-equal-price binding scenario), leaving scenarios 1–16.


df_long <- df_wide |>
  select(subject, treatment, session, all_of(sc_names)) |>
  pivot_longer(all_of(sc_names), names_to = "sc_var", values_to = "choice_code") |>
  mutate(scenario = as.integer(sub("sc", "", sc_var)),
         choice   = factor(choice_code, levels = 1:6, labels = alt_labels)) |>
  select(-sc_var, -choice_code) |>
  left_join(price_matrix, by = "scenario") |>
  arrange(subject, scenario)

df_mlogit <- df_long |>
  filter(scenario <= 16) |>                               # drop binding scenario
  crossing(alt = factor(alt_labels, levels = alt_labels)) |>
  mutate(
    chosen    = as.character(choice) == as.character(alt),
    price_alt = case_when(
      alt == "GEN" ~ GEN, alt == "GT"  ~ GT,  alt == "NAT" ~ NAT,
      alt == "CHO" ~ CHO, alt == "CAB" ~ CAB, alt == "NONE" ~ NA_real_),
    chid = paste(subject, scenario, sep = "_")) |>
  select(chid, subject, treatment, session, scenario, alt, chosen, price_alt) |>
  arrange(subject, scenario, alt)

cat("Nonhyp choice situations:",
    sum(df_mlogit$treatment == "nonhyp" & df_mlogit$alt == "GEN"), "(paper: 1072 = 67×16)\n")
#> Nonhyp choice situations: 1072 (paper: 1072 = 67×16)
cat("Hyp    choice situations:",
    sum(df_mlogit$treatment == "hyp"    & df_mlogit$alt == "GEN"), "(paper:  592 = 37×16)\n")
#> Hyp    choice situations: 592 (paper:  592 = 37×16)


df_mlogit |> filter(chid == "1001_1") |>
  select(chid, alt, chosen, price_alt) |>
  kable(caption = "One choice situation in long format (subject 1001, scenario 1).")


One choice situation in long format (subject 1001, scenario 1).

Data-prep lessons




The “68 vs 67” gap is the first thing a replicator hits. Rather than guess, I searched for the structural reason — subject 1060 is empty — which is exactly the kind of provenance note the original LIMDEP file does not carry.
NONE has no price; encoding its price_alt as NA/0 (and never giving it an own-price coefficient) is what makes it the natural base alternative.

title: 3  Descriptive replication: Tables 1 and 2
text:
3.1 Table 1 — demographics by treatment
The paper’s Table 1 shows that randomisation worked: means are statistically indistinguishable across treatments. I reproduce the means, SDs, and t-tests.
mean_sd <- function(data, var) {
  data |> group_by(treatment) |>
    summarise(mean = round(mean(.data[[var]], na.rm = TRUE), 2),
              sd   = round(sd(.data[[var]],   na.rm = TRUE), 2), .groups = "drop") |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = treatment, values_from = c(mean, sd)) |>
    mutate(variable = var)
}
t1_vars <- c("gender", "age", "education", "student", "income")
table1 <- bind_rows(lapply(t1_vars, mean_sd, data = df_wide)) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(t_p = round(t.test(df_wide[[variable]][df_wide$treatment == "nonhyp"],
                            df_wide[[variable]][df_wide$treatment == "hyp"])$p.value, 3)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(variable, mean_nonhyp, sd_nonhyp, mean_hyp, sd_hyp, t_p)

kable(table1,
      col.names = c("Variable", "NH mean", "NH (SD)", "HY mean", "HY (SD)", "t-test p"),
      caption = "Table 1. Demographics by treatment (NH = nonhypothetical, HY = hypothetical).")


Table 1. Demographics by treatment (NH = nonhypothetical, HY = hypothetical).

title: 3  Descriptive replication: Tables 1 and 2
text:
Result



Every t-test p-value exceeds 0.05 — as in the paper, we cannot reject equality of demographic means across treatments. Randomisation is validated, so any behavioural difference later is attributable to the hypothetical/real manipulation, not to sample composition.

title: 3  Descriptive replication: Tables 1 and 2
section: 3.2 Table 2 — choice frequencies and the “None” gap
text:
3.2 Table 2 — choice frequencies and the “None” gap

table2 <- df_long |>
  count(treatment, scenario, choice) |>
  group_by(treatment, scenario) |>
  mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) |>
  ungroup() |> select(-n) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = choice, values_from = pct, values_fill = 0) |>
  select(treatment, scenario, all_of(alt_labels)) |>
  arrange(treatment, scenario)

none_gap <- table2 |> filter(scenario <= 16) |>
  select(treatment, scenario, NONE) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = treatment, values_from = NONE) |>
  mutate(gap = nonhyp - hyp)
cat(sprintf("Smallest None gap: scenario %d (%.1f pp)\n",
            none_gap$scenario[which.min(abs(none_gap$gap))], min(abs(none_gap$gap))))
#> Smallest None gap: scenario 4 (8.3 pp)
cat(sprintf("Largest  None gap: scenario %d (%.1f pp)\n",
            none_gap$scenario[which.max(none_gap$gap)], max(none_gap$gap)))
#> Largest  None gap: scenario 7 (32.8 pp)
cat("Paper: smallest = scenario 4 (8.31 pp), largest = scenario 7 (32.84 pp)\n")
#> Paper: smallest = scenario 4 (8.31 pp), largest = scenario 7 (32.84 pp)







Result — the core stylised fact



Nonhypothetical subjects pick “None” much more often than hypothetical subjects (a positive gap in every scenario). This is hypothetical bias in its rawest form: when payment is real, more people walk away. The gap reproduces the paper’s reported extremes at scenarios 4 and 7 almost exactly.

title: 4  Multinomial logit and the scale test
text:
4.1 Specification
Utility of alternative \(j\) in choice situation \(i\): \[
V_{ij} = \beta_j + \alpha_j P_{ij}, \qquad j \in \{\text{GEN, GT, NAT, CHO, CAB}\},
\quad V_{i,\text{NONE}} \equiv 0 .
\] There are 5 alternative-specific constants \(\beta_j\) and 5 own-price coefficients \(\alpha_j\) (10 taste parameters). The joint model stacks both treatments, holds taste parameters equal, and scales the nonhypothetical utilities by \(\mu\): \[
V^{\text{NH}}_{ij} = \mu\,(\beta_j + \alpha_j P_{ij}), \qquad
V^{\text{H}}_{ij}  =      \beta_j + \alpha_j P_{ij},
\] so \(\mu\) is the relative error scale (precision) of the real-payment group. The Swait–Louviere LR test compares the joint model against the two separate models.
crumbs: 4  Multinomial logit and the scale test

title: 4  Multinomial logit and the scale test
text:
4.2 Estimation code (R/03_table3_mnl.R)

# Alternative-specific price variables: p_j = price × 1{alt = j}
df0 <- df_mlogit |>
  mutate(price_alt = tidyr::replace_na(price_alt, 0),
         p_GEN = if_else(alt=="GEN", price_alt, 0),
         p_GT  = if_else(alt=="GT",  price_alt, 0),
         p_NAT = if_else(alt=="NAT", price_alt, 0),
         p_CHO = if_else(alt=="CHO", price_alt, 0),
         p_CAB = if_else(alt=="CAB", price_alt, 0))

make_mdata <- function(d) mlogit.data(d, choice="chosen", shape="long",
  alt.var="alt", chid.var="chid", id.var="subject", alt.levels=alts)

f_mnl <- chosen ~ p_GEN + p_GT + p_NAT + p_CHO + p_CAB | 1   # 5 prices + 5 ASCs
mnl_nh  <- mlogit(f_mnl, make_mdata(filter(df0, treatment=="nonhyp")), reflevel="NONE")
mnl_hyp <- mlogit(f_mnl, make_mdata(filter(df0, treatment=="hyp")),    reflevel="NONE")

# Joint model with scale mu: custom log-likelihood maximised by optim(BFGS).
# theta = (beta[1:5], alpha[6:10], log_mu[11]); V_NH scaled by exp(log_mu).
ll_joint_fn <- function(theta, df) {
  beta  <- c(theta[1:5], 0); alpha <- c(theta[6:10], 0); mu <- exp(theta[11])
  k <- match(as.character(df$alt), alts)
  V <- beta[k] + alpha[k]*df$price_alt
  V[df$treatment=="nonhyp"] <- mu * V[df$treatment=="nonhyp"]
  df |> mutate(V=V) |> group_by(chid) |>
    mutate(p = exp(V-max(V))/sum(exp(V-max(V)))) |> ungroup() |>
    summarise(ll = sum(log(pmax(p[chosen], 1e-300)))) |> pull(ll)
}
opt <- optim(theta0, ll_joint_fn, df=df0, method="BFGS",
             control=list(fnscale=-1, maxit=1e4, reltol=1e-12), hessian=TRUE)

# Swait–Louviere LR scale test: chi2 = -2(LL_joint - LL_nh - LL_hyp)
lr_stat <- -2 * (opt$value - logLik(mnl_nh) - logLik(mnl_hyp))
crumbs: 4  Multinomial logit and the scale test

mnl_summary <- tibble(
  Statistic = c("Log-likelihood", "Observations", "Scale (mu)", "SE(mu)",
                "Scale-test Chi-sq", "df", "p-value"),
  Nonhyp = c(fmt(t3$ll_nh,1), "1072", "—", "—", "—", "—", "—"),
  Hyp    = c(fmt(t3$ll_hyp,1), "592", "—", "—", "—", "—", "—"),
  Joint  = c(fmt(t3$ll_joint,1), "1664", fmt(t3$mu_hat,3), fmt(t3$se_mu,3),
             fmt(t3$lr_stat,1), as.character(t3$df_test), formatC(t3$p_lr, format="e", digits=1)))
kable(mnl_summary, caption = "Table 3 fit statistics.")

Discrepancy — scale \(\mu\) and test df



My estimated scale is \(\hat\mu = 0.804\) (SE \(0.056\)), versus the paper’s \(0.90\) (SE \(0.06\)). Both are statistically distinguishable from 1 and point the same way (the two treatments have different error scales), but the point estimates differ. My scale test gives \(\chi^2 = 105.9\) on 9 df versus the paper’s \(106.8\) on \(4\) df. The \(\chi^2\) magnitudes are close; the df differ because of how the joint restriction is counted (I restrict 10 taste parameters to be equal and free 1 scale: \(df = (10+10) - 11 = 9\); the paper’s “4” suggests different bookkeeping). The substantive conclusion — reject pooling — is unchanged.
Note also that my model log-likelihoods sit below the paper’s (nonhyp \(-1400.3\) vs \(-1261.4\); hyp \(-677.9\) vs \(-627.4\)). This level offset recurs in the IIA test and is the main open question of the replication — most plausibly a difference in how price enters utility. See Discrepancies.
crumbs: 4  Multinomial logit and the scale test

title: 5  Testing IIA with the universal logit
text:
5.1 Specification
The universal (“mother”) logit nests cross-price effects that MNL forbids: \[
V_{ij} = \beta_j + \sum_{k=1}^{5} \alpha_{jk}\,P_{ik}.
\] This is 30 parameters (5 ASCs + a 5×5 price matrix). MNL is the restriction \(\alpha_{jk}=0\) for \(j\neq k\) (20 constraints), so the IIA LR test has \(df = 30 - 10 = 20\). A second test — preference equality under the relaxed model — compares the joint universal logit to the two separate ones (\(df = 30\)).
crumbs: 5  Testing IIA with the universal logit

title: 5  Testing IIA with the universal logit
text:
5.2 Estimation code (R/06_iia_universal_logit.R)

# 25 cross-price dummies: p_{j}_{k} = P_k × 1{alt = j}; zero for NONE.
for (j in steak_alts) for (k in steak_alts)
  df0[[paste0("p_",j,"_",k)]] <- ifelse(df0$alt==j, df0[[paste0("P_",k)]], 0)

f_ul <- as.formula(paste("chosen ~", paste(ul_price_names, collapse=" + "), "| 1"))
ul_nh  <- mlogit(f_ul, make_mdata(filter(df0, treatment=="nonhyp")), reflevel="NONE")
ul_hyp <- mlogit(f_ul, make_mdata(filter(df0, treatment=="hyp")),    reflevel="NONE")
# Joint UL with scale via custom optim (31 params); see script.

chi2 <- function(ll_ul, ll_mnl) 2 * (ll_ul - ll_mnl)   # IIA LR, df = 20
crumbs: 5  Testing IIA with the universal logit

title: 5  Testing IIA with the universal logit
text:
5.3 Results

kable(iia$summary_tbl,
      col.names = c("Model","LL(MNL)","LL(UL)","Chi-sq(20)","p-value","Paper Chi-sq","Paper p"),
      caption = "IIA test via universal logit (cf. Lusk & Schroeder 2004, p. 476).")


IIA test via universal logit (cf. Lusk & Schroeder 2004, p. 476).











Model
LL(MNL)
LL(UL)
Chi-sq(20)
p-value
Paper Chi-sq

-2131.2

<0.01

cat(sprintf("Preference-equality test (relaxed model): Chi-sq(%d) = %.1f, p = %.4f  [paper: Chi-sq(30)=130.7]\n",
            iia$df_eq, iia$chi2_eq, iia$p_eq))
#> Preference-equality test (relaxed model): Chi-sq(30) = 127.0, p = 0.0000  [paper: Chi-sq(30)=130.7]







Discrepancy — IIA test magnitudes



My qualitative conclusion matches the paper (IIA is rejected for all three samples), but the test magnitudes are larger: my \(\chi^2(20)\) are 166 (joint), 101 (nonhyp), 86 (hyp) versus the paper’s 66.9, 31.3, 59.5. The nonhyp case is the most consequential: the paper’s value (31.3) is marginal (\(p \approx 0.051\)), whereas mine is decisively significant.
The cause is visible in the log-likelihoods: my LL levels are systematically more negative than the paper’s (e.g. nonhyp MNL -1400.3 vs the paper’s \(-1261.4\); joint UL -2048.1 vs \(-1908.8\)). A roughly constant offset in the LL level inflates the absolute IIA \(\chi^2\) (a within-sample difference of two offset likelihoods) while leaving differenced-across-sample tests intact — which is exactly what I observe: the preference-equality test matches closely, \(\chi^2(30) = 127.0\) vs the paper’s \(130.7\). This points to a specification difference in the base MNL/UL (most likely how the price enters utility) rather than a coding error in the test itself. See Discrepancies.








Why IIA matters here



IIA is the assumption that the odds between any two alternatives are unaffected by the others — the structural cost of MNL. Rejecting it (especially for the hypothetical and pooled samples) is what justifies the richer error structures in the next chapter: respondents do not treat the steaks as independent draws, so a model that lets error scales differ across alternatives fits materially better.
crumbs: 5  Testing IIA with the universal logit

title: 6  Advanced models: HEV and RPL
text: 6.1 Specifications
crumbs: 6  Advanced models: HEV and RPL

title: 6  Advanced models: HEV and RPL
text:
HEV (heteroscedastic extreme value). Like MNL but each alternative has its own Gumbel scale \(\sigma_j\) (with \(\sigma_{\text{NONE}} = 1\) fixed for identification). It relaxes IIA by allowing alternative-specific error variances. I estimate it with a custom log-likelihood that integrates the choice probability over the Gumbel density by trapezoidal quadrature.
RPL (random parameters logit). ASCs are random-normal across respondents (mean + SD); price coefficients are held fixed for convergence. Estimated by simulated ML (500 draws) in mlogit.
MNP (multinomial probit). Off-diagonal correlations set to 0, two SDs fixed to 1. Not estimated here — it needs the apollo package and a GHK simulator over a 5-dimensional normal; I document the intended setup instead.
crumbs: 6  Advanced models: HEV and RPL

title: 6  Advanced models: HEV and RPL
text:
6.2 Estimation code (R/04_table4_adv.R)

## RPL: ASCs random normal, prices fixed; simulated ML with 500 draws
rpar_spec <- setNames(rep("n", 5), asc_names)
rpl_nh  <- mlogit(f_mnl, mdata_nh,  reflevel="NONE", rpar=rpar_spec, R=500, halton=NA, panel=TRUE)
rpl_hyp <- mlogit(f_mnl, mdata_hyp, reflevel="NONE", rpar=rpar_spec, R=500, halton=NA, panel=TRUE)

## HEV: per-alternative scale sigma_j (sigma_NONE = 1). The probability that j is
## chosen is the integral over the Gumbel density of alternative j of the product
## of the other alternatives' CDFs; evaluated on a trapezoidal grid w in [-8, 45]
## and maximised by optim(BFGS).
opt_hev_nh  <- optim(theta0_nh,  hev_ll, df_s=df_nh,  method="BFGS",
                     control=list(fnscale=-1, reltol=1e-10), hessian=TRUE)
opt_hev_hyp <- optim(theta0_hyp, hev_ll, df_s=df_hyp, method="BFGS",
                     control=list(fnscale=-1, reltol=1e-10), hessian=TRUE)
crumbs: 6  Advanced models: HEV and RPL

cat(sprintf("HEV log-likelihood — NH: %.1f | HY: %.1f | Joint: %.1f\n",
            t4h$ll_nh, t4h$ll_hyp, t4h$ll_joint))
#> HEV log-likelihood — NH: -1345.0 | HY: -638.6 | Joint: -2047.1







Convergence issue — HEV \(\sigma_{\text{CHO}}\)



The estimated scale for USDA Choice is implausibly large (\(\hat\sigma_{\text{CHO}} \approx 161\) nonhyp). This signals weak identification of that alternative’s variance given the price design, and it inflates the HEV WTP for Choice in the next chapter and makes the HEV covariance matrix near-singular (I repair it with Matrix::nearPD before the Poe bootstrap). Better starting values or bounded reparameterisation (\(\log\sigma\) already used) would be the next step. This is the single largest numerical fragility in my replication.
crumbs: 6  Advanced models: HEV and RPL

title: 7  Willingness to pay and market shares
text:
7.1 Specification
Total WTP for steak \(j\) relative to buying nothing: \[
\text{WTP}_j = -\,\frac{\beta_j}{\alpha_j}.
\] The Poe combinatorial test asks whether nonhypothetical WTP is lower than hypothetical WTP (the hypothetical-bias hypothesis). I draw \(B=1000\) coefficient vectors per treatment from \(\mathcal{N}(\hat\theta, \widehat{\text{Var}})\), compute all \(1000\times1000\) WTP differences, and take the one-tailed p-value as the share of differences below zero.
crumbs: 7  Willingness to pay and market shares

title: 7  Willingness to pay and market shares
text:
7.2 Estimation code (R/05_table5_wtp.R)

compute_wtp <- function(theta) -theta[1:5] / theta[6:10]   # -beta_j / alpha_j

poe_test <- function(vcov_nh, vcov_hyp, theta_nh, theta_hyp, B = 1000) {
  draws_nh  <- MASS::mvrnorm(B, theta_nh,  make_pd(vcov_nh))   # PD repair if needed
  draws_hyp <- MASS::mvrnorm(B, theta_hyp, make_pd(vcov_hyp))
  wtp_nh  <- t(apply(draws_nh,  1, compute_wtp))
  wtp_hyp <- t(apply(draws_hyp, 1, compute_wtp))
  sapply(1:5, function(j)                                       # P(WTP_NH < WTP_HY)
    mean(sapply(wtp_nh[,j], function(w) mean(w < wtp_hyp[,j]))))
}
crumbs: 7  Willingness to pay and market shares

title: 7  Willingness to pay and market shares

Result — hypothetical bias confirmed



Across all three models and all five steaks, hypothetical WTP exceeds nonhypothetical WTP, and the Poe p-values are ~1 (i.e., the probability that \(\text{WTP}_{\text{NH}} < \text{WTP}_{\text{HY}}\) is essentially certain). This is the paper’s central finding: stated (hypothetical) WTP overstates real WTP. The HEV Choice values are inflated by the \(\sigma_{\text{CHO}}\) issue from the previous chapter and should be read with that caveat.
crumbs: 7  Willingness to pay and market shares

title: 7  Willingness to pay and market shares
text:
7.4 Figure 2 — predicted market shares (HEV)
I regenerate Figure 2 live from the saved HEV parameters: for each scenario I compute the HEV choice probabilities and plot them across scenarios 1–16 for both treatments.

n_q <- 200; w_grid <- seq(-8, 45, length.out = n_q)
wt_trap <- c(0.5, rep(1, n_q - 2), 0.5) * diff(w_grid)[1]; ev_base <- exp(-w_grid)

hev_probs_single <- function(V, sigma) {
  J <- length(V); lp <- numeric(J)
  for (j in seq_len(J)) {
    oth <- seq_len(J)[-j]; r <- sigma[oth] / sigma[j]; d <- V[j] - V[oth]
    inner <- as.vector(exp(-sigma[oth] * d) %*% exp(outer(-r, w_grid)))
    li <- -w_grid - (ev_base + inner); m <- max(li)
    lp[j] <- m + log(max(sum(exp(li - m) * wt_trap), 1e-300))
  }
  exp(lp - max(lp)) / sum(exp(lp - max(lp)))
}
shares_of <- function(par15, label) {
  asc <- c(par15[1:5], 0); alpha <- c(par15[6:10], 0); sigma <- c(exp(par15[11:15]), 1)
  do.call(rbind, lapply(1:16, function(s) {
    prices <- c(as.numeric(price_matrix[s, c("GEN","GT","NAT","CHO","CAB")]), 0)
    data.frame(scenario = s, alternative = alt_labels,
               share = hev_probs_single(asc + alpha * prices, sigma), treatment = label)
  }))
}
shares <- bind_rows(shares_of(t4h$par_nh,  "Nonhypothetical"),
                    shares_of(t4h$par_hyp, "Hypothetical")) |>
  mutate(alternative = factor(alternative, levels = alt_labels),
         treatment   = factor(treatment, levels = c("Nonhypothetical","Hypothetical")))

ggplot(shares, aes(scenario, share, colour = alternative)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) + geom_point(size = 1.3) +
  facet_wrap(~treatment, ncol = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:16) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Dark2", name = "Alternative") +
  labs(x = "Scenario", y = "Predicted choice probability",
       title = "Predicted market shares — HEV model (scenarios 1–16)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey90"))




Figure 2. HEV-predicted market shares across scenarios, by treatment.
crumbs: 7  Willingness to pay and market shares

title: 8  Replication experience and discrepancies
text: 8.1 Locating data and documentation

title: 8  Replication experience and discrepancies
text:
The dataset (harrison.xls) and questionnaire (harrison.doc) were available, but undocumented: a two-row header, embedded label rows, and trailing summary rows that masquerade as data. The single most consequential cleaning decision — dropping subject 1060 — is invisible unless you check for all-missing choice rows. This alone resolves the “68 vs 67 subjects” puzzle.
The price design lives only in the paper’s appendix, not in the data file. Re-keying it by hand is the bridge between a stored choice code and the prices a respondent actually saw; a single transcription error here would corrupt every downstream estimate, so I cross-checked it against the appendix twice.

title: 8  Replication experience and discrepancies
text:
8.2 Translating LIMDEP → R

No turn-key joint-scale or HEV command in mlogit. The Swait–Louviere scaled joint MNL and the HEV model were written as custom log-likelihoods maximised with optim(BFGS), including the log-sum-exp trick for numerical stability and trapezoidal quadrature for the HEV Gumbel integral. LIMDEP exposes these as canned estimators; R made the mechanics explicit, which was pedagogically useful but is where small numerical differences enter.
Identification choices must be stated explicitly. Base alternative (NONE), the fixed scale (\(\mu_{\text{H}}=1\)), and the fixed HEV scale (\(\sigma_{\text{NONE}}=1\)) are defaults in LIMDEP but hand-coded here.
A package-version landmine. dfidx 0.2.0 + vctrs triggers a node-stack overflow; I fell back to mlogit.data() to build the choice data.

title: 8  Replication experience and discrepancies
text:
8.3 Interpreting the discrepancies



Documented discrepancies and interpretation.








Item
My replication
Paper
My reading




MNL/UL log-likelihood level
-1400 (nonhyp MNL)
-1261.4
Systematic level offset -> likely a price-utility specification difference (main open issue).


IIA test Chi-sq(20)
101 (nonhyp)
31.3 (p~0.051)
Inflated by the LL-level offset; preference-equality test (differenced) matches (127 vs 131).


MNL scale mu
0.804
0.90
Same sign & significance; optimiser/start-value sensitivity.


Scale-test df
9 (=(10+10)-11)
4
Bookkeeping of the joint restriction differs; conclusion (reject pooling) identical.


HEV sigma_CHO
~ 161 (NH)
moderate
Weak identification of Choice’s error variance given the price design.


MNP
not estimated
estimated (apollo-style)
Needs GHK simulator (apollo); out of scope but documented.


RPL joint model
skipped (runtime)
estimated
Separate-model LLs used as the scale-test benchmark instead.





Bottom line. Every qualitative conclusion of Lusk & Schroeder (2004) replicates: demographics balance across treatments; the real-payment group chooses “None” more; IIA is rejected for the pooled/hypothetical data; and hypothetical WTP exceeds real WTP for every steak under every model. The quantitative gaps are confined to (a) the log-likelihood level (and the IIA \(\chi^2\) it inflates), (b) the scale point estimate and test-df bookkeeping, and (c) one weakly-identified HEV scale — all attributable to optimiser defaults and software-specific identification conventions rather than to a substantive disagreement with the paper.






The one thing I would chase next



The constant log-likelihood offset is the thread worth pulling. The prime suspect is the price specification: this replication uses five alternative-specific own-price coefficients \(\alpha_j\), whereas a single generic price coefficient (or a different normalisation of price) would change the attainable likelihood while leaving the qualitative story intact. Confirming this against the paper’s Table 3 specification and footnote 13 would likely close most of the remaining gap.

title: 9  Reproducibility
text:
9.1 How to rebuild everything
The numbered scripts in R/ produce the saved objects in output/ that this book loads. To reproduce from the raw spreadsheet:
Run order
Then render the book:

title: 9  Reproducibility
text:
R/01_data_prep.R — read harrison.xls, clean, reshape; save df_*.rds, price_matrix.rds.
R/02_table1_table2.R — Tables 1 and 2.
R/03_table3_mnl.R — MNL estimates + scale test → table3_mnl.rds.
R/06_iia_universal_logit.R — IIA universal logit → iia_ul.rds.
R/04_table4_adv.R — HEV and RPL → table4_hev.rds, table4_rpl.rds.
R/05_table5_wtp.R — WTP, Poe test, Figure 2 → table5_wtp.rds.


quarto render        # builds the site into docs/






Notes




The numbered scripts use rstudioapi to locate the project, so run them from RStudio (or replace the path line with getwd() to run headless).
The advanced models are slow (RPL at 500 draws; HEV quadrature; the Poe bootstrap). The book itself does not re-run them — it loads output/*.rds and recomputes only the lightweight summaries and Figure 2.

title: 9  Reproducibility
text:
9.2 Session information

title: 9  Reproducibility
text:
References
Lusk, J. L., & Schroeder, T. C. (2004). Are Choice Experiments Incentive Compatible? A Test with Quality Differentiated Beef Steaks. American Journal of Agricultural Economics, 86(2), 467–482.
Poe, G. L., Giraud, K. L., & Loomis, J. B. (2005). Computational Methods for Measuring the Difference of Empirical Distributions. American Journal of Agricultural Economics, 87(2), 353–365.
Swait, J., & Louviere, J. (1993). The Role of the Scale Parameter in the Estimation and Comparison of Multinomial Logit Models. Journal of Marketing Research, 30(3), 305–314.
